key_insight: A two-step seismic workflow combining matched field processing and source relocation can map crevasse evolution on a glacier at metre-scale and reveal subcritical crack growth governed by viscous relaxation.
engineering_relevance: The method improves observation of fracture propagation in ice, helping constrain glacier damage mechanics, meltwater routing, and brittle-to-viscous transition behavior.
method: Use matched field processing to detect surface icequakes and meltwater noise with limited seismic instrumentation.
Relocate discrete arrivals to refine source positions to metre-scale accuracy.
Estimate crevasse propagation rates and infer diffusion coefficients from the relocated events.
evidence_signal: Applied to Hansbreen glacier data in Svalbard, the workflow identified ongoing crevasse opening episodes and yielded diffusion coefficients of 0.47 to 0.55 m²/s.
watch_item: Performance and source accuracy may degrade in sparse or irregular arrays, so careful array geometry and arrival picking are critical.
paper_title: Surface Crevasse Evolution Observed Using Matched Field Processing and Source Relocation at Hansbreen, Svalbard
